target.title: fix(ui): propagate connect errors to pending requests
target.body:
## Summary

Before this fix, pending RPC requests on WebSocket close were always flushed with a generic `gateway closed` error, discarding the structured connect error (e.g. `PAIRING_REQUIRED`, `AUTH_TOKEN_MISMATCH`). The UI could not show meaningful error messages because the error info was lost.

## Changes

- Extract `flushError` before both close/error call sites in `onWsClose`
- Create `GatewayRequestError` when `connectError` is available, preserving `gatewayCode` and `details`
- Fall back to generic `gateway closed` error when no structured error exists
- Add test: `flushes pending requests with structured connect errors on close`

## Test Plan

- [x] New test: verifies `PAIRING_REQUIRED` connect error propagates as `GatewayRequestError` with correct `gatewayCode` and `details`
- [x] Existing tests unchanged (error name, auto-reconnect behavior)
- [x] CI: check-test-types pass, dependency-guard-detect pass

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: When the Control UI WebSocket connection failed with a structured error (e.g. `INVALID_REQUEST` with `PAIRING_REQUIRED`), pending RPC requests were always flushed with a generic `Error("gateway closed (4008): connect failed")` instead of preserving the structured error. The UI could not distinguish between a random disconnect and an auth-required/pairing-needed state.

**Real environment tested**: Production OpenClaw Control UI (v2026.6.1, Node.js v22, Linux) connecting to a real Gateway instance with device auth enabled. Tested with both authenticated and unauthenticated device tokens.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

1. Started OpenClaw Gateway with device auth enabled
2. Opened Control UI with an invalid/unpaired device token
3. Sent a `cron.list` RPC request while the connection was still in connect phase
4. The gateway rejected the connect frame with `INVALID_REQUEST / PAIRING_REQUIRED`
5. Verified the pending `cron.list` request received a `GatewayRequestError` with `gatewayCode: "INVALID_REQUEST"` and `details: { code: "PAIRING_REQUIRED" }`
6. Confirmed the error rendered in the UI as an actionable "Pairing required" message instead of a generic "gateway closed" text

**Evidence after fix**:

```
Gateway server log (connect frame rejection):
gateway: connect frame from device abc123 rejected: INVALID_REQUEST (PAIRING_REQUIRED)

Control UI console (pending request rejection):
GatewayRequestError: INVALID_REQUEST
  gatewayCode: "INVALID_REQUEST"
  details: { code: "PAIRING_REQUIRED" }
    at GatewayBrowserClient.onWsClose (gateway.ts:520)
```

**Observed result after fix**:

- Pending requests now receive `GatewayRequestError` with structured error info when `connectError` is set
- `err.gatewayCode` is `"INVALID_REQUEST"` and `err.details.code` is `"PAIRING_REQUIRED"` — the UI can now render a specific message
- When no connect error exists (plain WebSocket drop), behavior is unchanged: generic `Error("gateway closed (1006): socket lost")`
- Unit test `flushes pending requests with structured connect errors on close` passes

**Not tested**: None for the behavior changed by this PR. Broader unrelated gateway/typecheck failures on current main are outside this PR scope.

target.title: Fix sessions.list for literal per-agent store paths
target.body:
## Summary
- treat literal `session.store` paths under `agents/<id>/sessions/sessions.json` as multi-agent discovery roots instead of single-store fallbacks
- keep the old single-store behavior for literal paths that do not match the per-agent on-disk layout
- add a regression covering the exact `.../agents/main/sessions/sessions.json` configuration from #54435

- `pnpm exec oxlint --type-aware src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest --run src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts -t "loadCombinedSessionStoreForGateway includes disk-only agents"` (run twice)

- Same API-backed proof script on this branch after the fix: `sessions.list` returned `count=2`

target.title: System prompt: add guidance for spawning background sub-agents
target.body:
Adds concrete system-prompt guidance for when to use sessions_spawn (background sub-agents), plus a small unit test covering the new section.

Greptile Overview

Greptile Summary

This PR updates the generated agent system prompt to include more concrete guidance on when to spawn background sub-agents (via `sessions_spawn`), and adds a unit test asserting the new section is present. The change lives in `src/agents/system-prompt.ts` within the Tooling section, which is included in full prompts produced by `buildAgentSystemPrompt`; the test in `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` exercises that the new guidance text is emitted.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Mostly safe to merge, but the prompt currently references a tool that may not be enabled in a given session. - Changes are localized to prompt text and a small test. The main concern is functional: `sessions_spawn` is named in guidance unconditionally, which can contradict the session’s enabled tool list and cause tool-call failures when `sessions_spawn` isn’t available. - src/agents/system-prompt.ts

target.title: UI: render half-block QR output in web chat
target.body:
## Summary
- Replaces the uneditable source branch from #51868 with a narrow implementation of the same user-facing fix.
- Detects UTF-8 half-block QR/block art and renders it with tight line-height, stable foreground/background colors, and horizontal overflow behavior that does not clip wide QR blocks.
- Covers raw tool/sidebar output and regular chat markdown so QR text does not need pre-existing triple backticks.
- Preserves leading/trailing QR quiet-zone whitespace when wrapping output.

## Credit
This carries forward the fix idea and reproduction from @emg110 in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/51868. Thanks @emg110 for the original report, implementation direction, and proof.

## Review findings addressed
- Greptile: keep half-block detection comments accurate and add sidebar pre/code font inheritance.
- Codex: detect raw half-block text before markdown turns it into paragraphs, preserve quiet-zone padding, and avoid clipping wide QR blocks behind code-block wrapper overflow.

## Validation
- pnpm -s vitest run ui/src/ui/markdown.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/tool-helpers.test.ts
- pnpm check:changed
- Codex /review must pass before opening the PR.

## Notes
No CHANGELOG.md edit is included because OpenClaw release generation owns changelog entries for normal PRs.

Clownfish 🐠 replacement reef notes:
- Cluster: repair-51868-qr-rendering
- Source PRs: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/51868
- Credit: Credit @emg110 for the original fix idea and source PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/51868 in the replacement PR body.; Do not edit CHANGELOG.md for the normal PR; OpenClaw release generation owns changelog entries. If maintainers add release notes later, the entry should preserve @emg110 attribution.; The replacement must not carry unrelated commits or broad drift from GoPlausible/openclaw:main.

target.title: fix(cron): honor configured retry.backoffMs for recurring error backoff floor
target.body:
## Summary

For recurring (`every`/`cron`) cron jobs that error, the safety-net backoff floor that sets `nextRunAtMs` was computed with the hardcoded `DEFAULT_ERROR_BACKOFF_SCHEDULE_MS`, **silently ignoring the operator-configured `cronConfig.retry.backoffMs`**. Every sibling path handling the same recurring-error backoff window passes the configured schedule (`errorBackoffMs(..., retryConfig.backoffMs)` on the retry-window branch; `cronConfig?.retry?.backoffMs ?? DEFAULT...` at the `isErrorBackoffPending` / `deferPendingBackoffMissedCronSlots` scheduler gates). Only the recurring safety-net floor in `applyJobResult` diverged. Result: the persisted `nextRunAtMs` is computed on the default schedule while the gate that decides runnability uses the configured one, so they disagree (early wasted wake-ups when config > default; over-long delays when config < default).

## What changed

- `applyJobResult` now passes the configured schedule to the recurring-error backoff floor: `errorBackoffMs(job.state.consecutiveErrors ?? 1, state.deps.cronConfig?.retry?.backoffMs ?? DEFAULT_ERROR_BACKOFF_SCHEDULE_MS)` — matching the sibling gate paths and the documented `cron.retry.backoffMs` behavior for recurring jobs.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** a recurring cron job that errors used the hardcoded default backoff floor for its persisted `nextRunAtMs`, ignoring `cronConfig.retry.backoffMs`; the scheduler gate enforced the configured window, so persisted next-run and gate disagreed.
- **Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw from source on Linux. The proof drives the real cron service execution path end-to-end — `run(state, jobId, "due")` → `executeJobCoreWithTimeout` (real `runIsolatedAgentJob` throwing a permanent error) → `applyJobResult` → `persist` into the SQLite-backed store — then reads `nextRunAtMs` **back from SQLite** via `loadCronStore`. Nothing on the backoff/persistence path is mocked; only the agent runner throws, to produce the error result.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.backoff-config-readback.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix (terminal capture):**
  ```
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.backoff-config-readback.test.ts
   ✓ persists the configured retry.backoffMs floor across a real run and SQLite readback
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  1 passed (1)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** with `cronConfig.retry.backoffMs=[300000]` and an `every`-1s job hit by a non-retryable permanent error, the value **read back from the SQLite store** is `nextRunAtMs === endedAt + 300000` (configured floor), with `lastStatus === "error"` and `consecutiveErrors === 1`, instead of `endedAt + 30000` (hardcoded default).
- **What was not tested:** a long-lived multi-tick scheduler daemon across process restarts; the proof drives a single real service run and verifies the durable SQLite state rather than an in-memory value.
- **Negative control:** reverting `timer.ts` to the default-only floor (`git checkout eefeab5766^ -- src/cron/service/timer.ts`) and re-running the test fails with `AssertionError: expected 1772452830000 to be 1772453100000` — i.e. SQLite persisted `endedAt+30000` instead of `endedAt+300000` — confirming the test is not a tautology and the fix is required. Restoring the fix turns it green.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.backoff-config-readback.test.ts` → 1 passed (real `run()` execution + SQLite readback).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.backoff-config.test.ts` → 1 passed (unit-level `applyJobResult` floor).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` → 63 passed (no regression).
- `tsgo:core:test` clean on changed files; `oxfmt --write` clean.

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior change? Yes (a fix) — recurring cron error backoff now honors `cron.retry.backoffMs`.
- Config/env/migration change? No.
- Security/auth/secrets/network/tool-execution change? No.
- Highest-risk area: changing the persisted next-run for errored recurring jobs; covered by the new SQLite-readback test, the unit floor test, and the retained 63 regression tests.

target.title: perf(memory): prewarm explicit local embeddings on gateway startup
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: builtin `memory_search` with explicit `memorySearch.provider = "local"` still pays model/context initialization on the first post-startup search because gateway startup only warmed QMD.
- Why it matters: the first memory-backed reply after a gateway restart absorbs the local embedding cold start instead of the post-attach startup path.
- What changed: extend `startGatewayMemoryBackend` to prewarm builtin local managers by calling `probeEmbeddingAvailability()` after manager creation, and add gateway startup tests for explicit-local, skip, and failure logging paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not prewarm `provider = "auto"` or remote embedding providers, so startup avoids new remote network calls or auth/cost side effects.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #70460
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: builtin memory with explicit `memorySearch.provider = "local"` now enters the Gateway startup prewarm path instead of waiting for the first real `memory_search`; prewarm failures are warnings and do not block startup.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout, production build output from `pnpm build`, temporary OpenClaw state/workspace, Node invoking the built `dist/server-startup-memory-*.js` module.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the built startup module with a temporary config using `memory.backend=builtin`, `agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider=local`, vector disabled, and a temporary `memory/MEMORY.md` file.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
proof module: server-startup-memory-DNxA_AEE.js
workspace: <temp>/workspace
warn: builtin local memory startup prewarm failed for agent "main": Error: Local embeddings unavailable.
Reason: optional dependency node-llama-cpp is missing (or failed to install).
Detail: Cannot find package 'node-llama-cpp' imported from /Users/tsy/opensouce/openclaw/dist/memory-core-host-engine-embeddings-B374_HhW.js
To enable local embeddings:
1) Use Node 24 (recommended for installs/updates; Node 22 LTS, currently 22.16+, remains supported)
2) Install node-llama-cpp next to the OpenClaw package or source checkout
3) If you use pnpm: pnpm approve-builds (select node-llama-cpp), then pnpm rebuild node-llama-cpp
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "github-copilot" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "openai" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "gemini" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "voyage" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "mistral" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "deepinfra" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "bedrock" (remote).
result: startup hook completed with 1 log event(s)
```

- Observed result after fix: the production startup hook called the builtin local prewarm path for agent `main`, surfaced the real local-provider availability error as a warning, and completed without throwing or blocking startup.
- What was not tested: successful local model load with `node-llama-cpp` installed on this machine; the real setup here verifies the startup/prewarm routing and graceful failure path.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, builtin backends were skipped by `startGatewayMemoryBackend` unless the resolved backend was QMD, so this same builtin/local startup path did not call the memory manager prewarm probe.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: post-attach startup only initialized QMD memory, so builtin local memory waited until the first real search to create the manager and load the local embedding context.
- Missing detection / guardrail: gateway startup coverage did not assert builtin explicit-local prewarm behavior or failure logging.
- Contributing context (if known): `purpose: "status"` managers bypass the builtin cache, so a status-only probe would not have helped the first real search.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-startup-memory.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: explicit local builtin memory runs `probeEmbeddingAvailability()` at startup, non-local builtin configs skip startup work, and probe failures degrade to warnings.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the gateway startup decision point without requiring real local model assets.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing QMD startup tests in the same file.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Gateways using builtin memory with `agents.*.memorySearch.provider = "local"` now shift local embedding cold-start work into post-attach startup, so the first real `memory_search` request avoids paying that initialization cost.
- No behavior change for QMD, `provider = "auto"`, or remote memory embedding providers.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
gateway boot -> first builtin local memory_search -> init local embedding context -> delayed first result

After:
gateway boot -> post-attach startup prewarm -> first builtin local memory_search -> reuse warmed context
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js via `pnpm`
- Model/provider: builtin memory with explicit local embedding provider
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `memory.backend=builtin`, `agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider=local`

### Steps

1. Start the gateway with builtin memory and an explicit local memory provider.
2. Let post-attach startup run.
3. Trigger the first `memory_search` request after startup.

### Expected

- The first real search reuses a manager whose local embedding provider has already been prewarmed.
- Non-local builtin configs still skip startup warmup.
- Failed prewarm attempts only log warnings.

### Actual

- Before this change, only QMD was warmed at startup, so builtin local memory paid the cold-start cost on the first real search.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `pnpm test src/gateway/server-startup-memory.test.ts`; `pnpm build`
- Edge cases checked: builtin non-local skip path, builtin local probe failure logging, existing QMD startup path
- What you did **not** verify: end-to-end timing against a real local model cache/download on a fresh gateway boot

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: explicit local memory configs now do extra startup work and could log a warning earlier in the boot cycle.
- Mitigation: the prewarm runs only for `memory.backend=builtin` plus explicit `provider=local`, after post-attach startup, and failures degrade to warnings without blocking startup.

target.title: add option for MCP apps support
target.body:
## Summary
 - Problem: OpenClaw calls MCP servers, but ignores MCP Apps UI metadata/resources
 - Why it matters: MCP Apps turns trusted MCP servers into richer tool providers with purpose-built UI. Without host support, OpenClaw misses that part of the MCP ecosystem even when the server is configured and callable.
- What changed: Added opt-in MCP Apps host support: parse UI metadata, hide app-only tools from the model, fetch MCP App resources into canvas documents, render them through the existing embed/canvas path with an app bridge, and proxy app-initiated MCP calls through the active session runtime.
- What changed: Added restart/reload hardening for restored app views, wider assistant canvas layout, docs, config schema support,tests, and a security-audit warning when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
-  What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal MCP tool execution still works without MCP Apps. MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled`; this does not add a marketplace, new MCP server examples, or plugin-only architecture.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- MCP app-only tools are not materialized for the model.
- MCP Apps UI payloads are only fetched/rendered when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
- App bridge calls are rejected when MCP Apps are disabled.
- App bridge calls enforce app-callable tool visibility.
- Persisted/restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
- Existing `[embed]` canvas rendering remains the fallback path when MCP Apps metadata is absent or disabled.

- Target test or file:
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations. 
- Scenario the test should lock in:
    - MCP Apps metadata is preserved from MCP tool catalogs through tool results and chat rendering.
    - App-only tools are hidden from the model.
    - Model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
    - MCP Apps UI rendering and bridge proxy calls require `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
    - Restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
    - Existing canvas/embed rendering remains the fallback when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
    - The feature crosses runtime materialization, gateway proxying, config/bootstrap, and UI rendering. Focused unit and seam tests cover those boundaries without requiring a live browser, real MCP server fleet, or container e2e in normal CI.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
    - Existing canvas/embed tests covered generic iframe rendering, but not MCP Apps metadata, app-only visibility, app bridge calls, or restored MCP runtime behavior.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations.
  
## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```mermaid
  flowchart LR
    A[MCP server tool] --> B[Tool result with UI metadata]
    B --> C[OpenClaw MCP runtime]
    C --> D[Fetch MCP App HTML resource]
    D --> E[Canvas document / existing embed path]
    E --> F[Control UI mcp-app-view iframe]
    F --> G[AppBridge]
    G --> H[Gateway mcp.* proxy]
    H --> C
  ```
## Security Impact (required)
  - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
  - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No for normal MCP transport; Yes in the sense that enabled MCP Apps can make app-initiated MCP calls back through the existing MCP session runtime.
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
    - Risks:
       - MCP servers with MCP Apps support can provide HTML/JS UI resources that OpenClaw renders in chat.
       -  rendered app views can call app-visible MCP tools and read MCP resources through the active session runtime.
       - app views receive the tool input/result payload they are rendering.
    - Mitigations:
        - MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
        - app views render in sandboxed iframes using the existing Control UI embed sandbox policy.
        - app-only tools are hidden from the model, and model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
        - app bridge calls are scoped to the active MCP session/server and exposed through narrow `mcp.*` gateway methods.
        - `openclaw security audit` warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
- Trust model: enabling MCP Apps means trusting the configured MCP servers not only as tool providers, but also as UI code providers.

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment
  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: local OpenClaw container via Podman
  - Model/provider: Claude Sonnet 4.6
  - Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat with configured MCP servers
  - Relevant config (redacted):

```json
  {
    "mcp": {
      "apps": {
        "enabled": true
      },
      "servers": {
        "system-monitor": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-system-monitor", "--stdio"]
        },
        "pdf": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-pdf", "--stdio"]
        },
        "qr": {
          "url": "http://127.0.0.1:3108/mcp",
          "transport": "streamable-http"
        }
      }
    }
  }
```

### Steps
  1. Build and run an OpenClaw image from this branch.
  2. Enable MCP Apps with openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json.
  3. Configure the system-monitor, pdf, and qr MCP servers.
  4. Start the QR MCP sidecar if using the QR example.
  5. Start a fresh chat/session in the Control UI.
  6. Prompt the agent to show live system info using the system-monitor MCP Apps UI.
  7. Prompt the agent to generate a QR code using the QR MCP Apps UI.
  8. Prompt the agent to display https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.03762 using the PDF MCP Apps UI.
  9. Restart the OpenClaw container and reload the Control UI.
  10. Confirm the persisted PDF app view reloads and reconnects.

### Expected

  - Normal MCP tools still work.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=true, MCP Apps UI resources render as interactive embedded app views.
  - System monitor renders a live monitor UI.
  - QR renders an embedded QR UI.
  - PDF renders an interactive PDF viewer.
  - Restored app views can reconnect after gateway/container restart.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=false, app bridge calls are rejected and MCP Apps render through the normal fallback path instead of the app bridge.

### Actual

  - Verified locally with system-monitor, QR, and PDF MCP Apps examples.
  - Verified upstream image comparison: upstream can call the MCP servers but does not render the MCP Apps UI resources.
  - Verified restart/reload behavior for the PDF app view.

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aaa93975-4406-493e-a060-4ac64763704b

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
    - No migration is required for existing installs. MCP Apps support is default-off.
    - To enable it, set:
      ```bash
      openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json
      ```
    - Configure MCP servers that expose MCP Apps / `mcp-ui` resources.
    - Restart OpenClaw so the updated config is loaded.

## Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: Enabling MCP Apps allows configured MCP servers to provide HTML/JS UI that renders inside OpenClaw.
    - Mitigation: Feature is opt-in via `mcp.apps.enabled`; app UI renders in a sandboxed iframe; docs and security audit call out the trust boundary.
  - Risk: App views can call app-visible MCP tools/resources through the bridge, increasing the practical surface area of a trusted MCP server.
    - Mitigation: Bridge is scoped to MCP app methods only; model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls; app-only tools are hidden from the model.
  - Risk: Restored chat views may need to reconnect to MCP runtimes after a container restart.
    - Mitigation: Gateway proxy lazily recreates the session MCP runtime from config when an app bridge call arrives and no runtime is currently active.
  - Risk: Servers without MCP Apps support, or installs with the feature disabled, should not regress.
    - Mitigation: Existing MCP tool execution path remains unchanged; UI falls back to the existing embed/canvas rendering behavior when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.

target.title: fix(infra): tolerate deleted cwd across startup, PATH, home-dir, and TUI [AI-assisted]
target.body:
> [AI-assisted] This PR was generated using Claude Code.

## Summary

**Problem:** When the current working directory is deleted (by another process, `git clean`, or directory removal), the OpenClaw CLI/TUI crashes with `Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, uv_cwd` because multiple startup **and runtime** code paths call `process.cwd()` without handling the `ENOENT` error.

**Why it matters:** Hard crash with no recovery — the user sees only the raw `uv_cwd` error. The CLI becomes unusable until the user manually `cd`s to an existing directory. Per maintainer review, the original version of this PR missed the TUI runtime path (`runTui`, `tui-local-shell`) and used a single generic `HOME` fallback that changed project-local PATH trust semantics.

**What changed (rework per review):** Dropped the generic `safeCwd()→HOME` helper in favor of **nullable cwd handling** with **caller-specific** fallback behavior, adopting the design endorsed in PR #93034:
- New `src/infra/safe-cwd.ts`: `tryProcessCwd(): string | null` + `resolveProcessCwdOrFallback(fallback)`.
- **dotenv** (`cli/dotenv`, `cli/gateway-dispatch-dotenv`, `cli/run-main`, `infra/dotenv`): skip workspace `.env` when cwd is deleted (it cannot exist); global `.env` loading continues.
- **PATH** (`infra/path-env`): `if (allowProjectLocalBin && cwd)` — skip project-local bin lookup when cwd is null, so `$HOME/node_modules/.bin` is never treated as the deleted project path.
- **required-home** (`infra/home-dir`): fall back to `path.dirname(process.execPath)` — not `os.tmpdir()` (shared-temp state-write surface) and not `os.homedir()` (already empty/throwing in this branch, so `path.resolve(os.homedir())` would reach back into `process.cwd()` and crash).
- **TUI** (`tui/tui.ts`): `runTui` resolves a nullable `launchCwd` once; `resolveInitialTuiAgentId` skips workspace inference when cwd is gone; `resolveLocalAuthSpawnCwd`, the autocomplete provider cwd, and the auth-login spawn fall back to the CLI wrapper's directory.
- **tui-local-shell** (`tui/tui-local-shell.ts`): the `!cmd` local shell spawn falls back to `os.homedir()`.
- **is-main** (`infra/is-main.ts`): aligned to `resolveProcessCwdOrFallback(path.dirname(currentFile))`, removing a duplicated try/catch.

**What did NOT change:** Config/env loading semantics for the normal (cwd present) case; global `.env` / state-dir behavior; the `project-local-bin` default (still off unless `OPENCLAW_ALLOW_PROJECT_LOCAL_BIN` is set).

## Linked context

Closes #73676
Related: #74994 (previous failed attempt — `os.tmpdir()` PATH-boundary issue), #93034 (endorsed nullable-cwd repair base; this PR adopts its design and diverges only on the required-home fallback)

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: CLI/TUI crash with `uv_cwd` ENOENT when working directory is deleted, across startup dotenv, PATH bootstrap, home-dir resolution, and TUI startup/runtime paths.
- Real environment tested: Windows 10 Enterprise (Node v22.17.1), OpenClaw repo checkout on `fix/vuln-73676`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/infra/safe-cwd.test.ts src/infra/home-dir.test.ts` — 32/32 pass (incl. deleted-cwd regression tests)
  2. `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/infra/dotenv.test.ts` — 35/35 pass (incl. "skips workspace .env and still loads global env when cwd was deleted", "still loads global CLI env when cwd was deleted")
  3. `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cli/run-main.test.ts` — 40/40 pass
  4. `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/tui/tui.test.ts src/tui/tui-local-shell.test.ts` — 67/67 pass (incl. TUI + local-shell deleted-cwd fallback tests)
  5. `pnpm tsgo`, `oxlint`, `oxfmt --check` on changed files — all pass
- Evidence after fix: deleted-cwd branches are exercised by focused tests that force `process.cwd()` to throw the same `ENOENT: uv_cwd` shape and assert each caller either skips (dotenv/PATH/agent-inference) or falls back (home-dir → execPath dir, TUI → wrapper dir, local-shell → `os.homedir()`) instead of crashing.
- Observed result after fix: startup and TUI runtime paths no longer crash when CWD is deleted; workspace `.env` and project-local PATH lookups are correctly skipped; global fallbacks continue to work.
- What was not tested:
  - Full `pnpm build` on Windows (pre-existing `write-cli-startup-metadata` failure unrelated to this change)
  - `pnpm check:host-env-policy:swift` (requires Swift, not available on Windows)
  - Literal `cd deleted-dir && openclaw tui` on Linux/macOS — Windows locks the CWD while a process holds it, preventing in-process deletion; the bug manifests on Linux/macOS where another process can `rmdir` the CWD, and is exercised via the focused tests that mock `process.cwd()` to throw.
- Before evidence: CLI exits with `Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, uv_cwd` at startup (and `openclaw tui` crashes post-startup) when CWD is deleted.

## Tests and validation

- Commands run:
  - `oxfmt --check` on changed files ✅
  - `pnpm tsgo` ✅
  - `oxlint` on changed files ✅
  - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/infra/safe-cwd.test.ts` ✅ (4/4)
  - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/infra/home-dir.test.ts` ✅ (28/28)
  - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/infra/dotenv.test.ts` ✅ (35/35)
  - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cli/run-main.test.ts` ✅ (40/40)
  - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/tui/tui.test.ts src/tui/tui-local-shell.test.ts` ✅ (67/67)
  - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/infra/path-env.test.ts` — 13 pass incl. new "skips project-local bin fallback when cwd was deleted"; 2 pre-existing Linuxbrew/macOS failures on main, unrelated to this change.

- Regression coverage added: deleted-cwd tests for every changed caller — `safe-cwd.test.ts` (null + fallback), `home-dir.test.ts` (required-home → execPath dir), `dotenv.test.ts` (workspace skip + global still loads, for both `loadDotEnv` and `loadCliDotEnv`), `path-env.test.ts` (project-local bin skipped), `tui.test.ts` (agent inference falls back), `tui-local-shell.test.ts` (spawn cwd → `os.homedir()`).

- What failed before this fix: unguarded `process.cwd()` call sites in startup dotenv, PATH bootstrap, home-dir resolution, and TUI (`runTui`, `resolveLocalAuthSpawnCwd`, `resolveInitialTuiAgentId`, `tui-local-shell`) would throw `ENOENT` and crash with no recovery.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes` — CLI/TUI no longer crash when CWD is deleted; instead it gracefully skips project-local lookups or falls back to a caller-appropriate directory.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No` — config loading falls through to the same global `.env` / state-dir paths when workspace `.env` is skipped.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No` — PATH resolution does not gain a `HOME/node_modules/.bin` or shared-temp entry; the project-local-bin feature remains off by default. The required-home fallback (`path.dirname(process.execPath)`) is a non-shared existing directory.

What is the highest-risk area? `src/infra/path-env.ts` — `candidateBinDirs()` now uses a nullable cwd. This only affects the `allowProjectLocalBin` path (opt-in via `OPENCLAW_ALLOW_PROJECT_LOCAL_BIN`), and the change makes it *safer* (skip vs. the prior HOME-substitution).

How is that risk mitigated? `project-local-bin` is off by default; the deleted-cwd case now skips the lookup entirely rather than substituting a wrong directory. Covered by a dedicated regression test.

## Current review state

Reworked per maintainer review (TUI coverage + caller-specific nullable-cwd fallbacks). Awaiting re-review, in particular on the required-home fallback choice (`path.dirname(process.execPath)`).

target.title: feat: fire session reset hooks for daily and idle resets
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Internal hooks and `before_reset` plugin hook only fire for manual `/new` and `/reset` commands, not for daily (4AM) or idle-timeout session resets. This means `session-memory` never saves on daily/idle resets.
- Why it matters: 4 community issues report lost session memory on daily/idle resets (#10142, #31266, #50891, #43524)
- What changed: Wire both hook types into the lazy staleness path in `initSessionState()`, update `session-memory` registration and filter
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new hook event names, no changes to plugin SDK API, no extraction/refactor — only `export` added to existing functions

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Memory / storage

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #10142
- Closes #31266
- Closes #50891
- Closes #43524
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `initSessionState()` fires `session_end`/`session_start` plugin hooks for lazy resets but never fires `triggerInternalHook` or `hookRunner.runBeforeReset`. These only fire via `emitResetCommandHooks()` in `commands-core.ts`, which is only called for manual `/new` and `/reset` commands.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test asserted that internal hooks fire for lazy resets
- Contributing context (if known): The lazy evaluation design means sessions aren't proactively killed — staleness is checked on next message arrival, a code path separate from manual commands

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/session.stale-hooks.test.ts` (new), `src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts` (extended)
- Scenario the test should lock in: Internal hook fires with `action: "daily"` / `"idle"` when session is stale; `before_reset` plugin hook fires for lazy resets; no double-fire on manual reset
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Mocks `triggerInternalHook` and `hookRunner` to assert dispatch without side-effects
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `session-hooks-context.test.ts` covers `session_end`/`session_start` but not internal hooks or `before_reset`

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`session-memory` now saves session summaries on daily (4AM) and idle-timeout resets, not just manual `/new` and `/reset`. Plugin `before_reset` hook now fires for lazy resets, giving plugins pre-archive transcript access.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[lazy reset] -> session_end + session_start hooks only

After:
[lazy reset] -> internal hook (command:daily/idle) + before_reset + session_end + session_start
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Any
- Runtime/container: Node.js 24
- Integration/channel (if any): Any channel with daily or idle reset configured

### Steps

1. Configure a session with daily reset (default 4AM)
2. Have an active session with messages before 4AM
3. Send a message after 4AM (triggering lazy staleness detection)

### Expected

- `session-memory` saves a summary of the previous session
- `before_reset` plugin hook fires with `reason: "daily"` and transcript messages

### Actual (before this PR)

- `session-memory` does not fire — no summary saved
- `before_reset` plugin hook does not fire

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Daily reset with stale session triggers session-memory save; idle reset triggers save; manual /new does not double-fire
- Edge cases checked: First-ever session (no previous entry), system events (heartbeat), fresh session
- What you did **not** verify: End-to-end with real LLM slug generation (mocked in tests)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `before_reset` receives the post-archive `sessionFile` path (after `renameSync`), not the original path. `loadBeforeResetTranscript` handles ENOENT by scanning for the latest `.reset.*` archived sibling.
  - Mitigation: This matches the manual reset path behavior. The stable post-archive path is intentional — it avoids racing transcript reads against the archive rename.

## AI Disclosure

- [x] Mark as AI-assisted in the PR title or description
- [x] Note the degree of testing: fully tested — 10 unit tests (7 new + 3 extended) + manual daily/idle reset verification
- [x] Confirm: I understand what this code does and have verified the hook dispatch logic, guard conditions, and fire-and-forget patterns

🤖 AI-assisted with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

cc @vincentkoc (hooks/plugins CODEOWNER)

target.title: # PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)
target.body:
# PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)

## Summary

This PR introduces a stricter, policy-driven filesystem access layer (**PathGuard**) for OpenClaw agent filesystem tools, including local media (image/pdf) loaders.

It closes key security and consistency gaps by:
- canonicalizing requested paths before evaluation (realpath-aware, ENOENT-safe),
- preventing traversal and symlink/junction escapes,
- enforcing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths` consistently for classic filesystem tools **and** local media loads,
- preserving **deny-overrides-allow** semantics,
- anchoring workspace-relative policy entries to the workspace root (and re-anchoring after realpath),
- treating `allowedPaths: []` as an explicit **deny-all**,
- supporting absolute glob policy entries (via minimatch) alongside canonical absolute literals,
- returning structured, remediation-focused policy denial tool results (`policy_denied`) when access is blocked,
- and enforcing a monotonic filesystem policy ceiling for spawned subagents (global + agent + subagent defaults + spawn-time tightening), plumbed end-to-end into the spawned run.

**Important scope note:**

R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”

Remote URLs (`http(s)://`) and inline `data:` inputs are not treated as filesystem paths and are outside this layer.

---

## Change Type

- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Tests

---

## Linked Issues

- Closes #39672
- Related #8719

---

## Problem Statement (What was wrong before)

Before this PR, filesystem tool enforcement had multiple gaps:

1) **Path canonicalization and symlink escape handling were fragmented**
- Traversal protections and “what path are we actually touching?” logic were spread across tools/flows.
- Non-existent paths (ENOENT) were tricky: naive realpath calls fail and can allow later-creation escapes.

2) **Policy matching was inconsistent**
- Absolute vs relative entries could be evaluated in different “path spaces”.
- Glob vs non-glob entries could diverge in behavior, especially when “roots” filtering was used as an approximation.

3) **Media tools could diverge from file tool policy**
- Local file references inside image/pdf flows could reopen outside-workspace reads if not enforced per resolved file.

4) **Sandbox and host flows lacked a unified enforcement story**
- Enforcement needed to be identical regardless of whether the run is sandboxed or host-based.

5) **Spawned subagents did not have a guaranteed filesystem policy ceiling**
- Subagents needed an explicit, monotonic ceiling that could not be loosened at spawn time.

---

## What Changed (Design + Implementation)

### 1) Core PathGuard enforcement (`src/security/path-guard.ts`)

PathGuard implements:

- **Canonical resolution for existing paths** via `fs.realpath`.
- **Nearest-existing-parent resolution** for ENOENT paths (prevents “create later via symlink” bypass).
- **Symlink/junction-aware comparisons** in canonical path space.
- **Deny precedence**: deny rules always override allow rules.
- **Workspace-only boundary**: `workspaceOnly` blocks outside-workspace access.
- **Relative entry anchoring**: workspace-relative policy entries are anchored to the workspace root, and re-anchored after realpath resolution.
- **Empty allowlists**: `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit deny-all.
- **Absolute policy entries**:
  - absolute **literals** are canonicalized before comparison,
  - absolute **globs** are supported (matched against canonical real paths) without attempting to realpath the pattern.
- **Glob detection** via `Minimatch(...).hasMagic()` (brace/extglob supported).

### 2) Filesystem tool integration (read/write/edit/apply_patch-style)

- Tool FS config is resolved through the tool/fs policy configuration and enforced using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`.
- Wrappers normalize args and run PathGuard checks on resolved paths.
- `apply_patch` is explicitly guarded by parsing patch hunks and enforcing FS policy on each patch target path (not just args.path/file_path).

### 3) Structured `policy_denied` tool result (model guidance)

When PathGuard denies access, tools return a structured tool result (instead of only throwing a raw error) that:
- states the restriction is system-enforced and cannot be bypassed,
- describes what the tool attempted to do,
- provides actionable remediation options,
- and explicitly points to config keys that can be changed to grant access.

This improves model behavior: fewer useless retries, more direct user remediation.

### 4) Media tool integration (image/pdf local paths)

Local media loads are now policy-checked on the resolved file path:
- `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`
- `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.ts`

Key invariant:
- enforcement is per resolved path using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`, not via root-prefix approximations.

### 5) Sandbox behavior

For local media loads in sandbox mode:
- PathGuard runs in sandbox flows too,
- policy root uses `sandbox.root` when sandboxed,
- otherwise `workspaceDir` is used.

### 6) Subagent filesystem policy ceiling (spawn-time tightening + runtime enforcement)

Goal: spawned subagents must never receive broader filesystem access than the configured ceiling.

Policy sources (monotonic tightening):
- Global: `tools.fs`
- Per-agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- Subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- Sessions-spawn defaults/ceiling: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (applied when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`)
- Spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge semantics:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: enforced as a ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

Persistence + plumbing:
- Effective policy is persisted on the spawned child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) via `sessions.patch`.
- Gateway plumbs it into the child run as ingress field `toolFsPolicy`.
- Gateway session resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve `spawnedToolFsPolicy` (no widening after reset).
- Embedded runner passes it into tool construction.
- `createOpenClawCodingTools` accepts an `fsPolicy` override and uses it as the effective policy for that run.
- Nested spawns inherit the parent session’s `spawnedToolFsPolicy` ceiling before applying config/tool-call tightening.

---

## Configuration (for contributors)

### Filesystem policy knobs

- `tools.fs.workspaceOnly: boolean`
- `tools.fs.allowedPaths: string[] | undefined` (empty array means deny-all)
- `tools.fs.denyPaths: string[] | undefined`

Subagent ceiling:
- `tools.subagents.fs.{workspaceOnly, allowedPaths, denyPaths}`

Sessions-spawn defaults + spawn-time tightening:
- `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (config default ceiling/tightening)
- `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (tool arg tightening; can only further restrict)

Notes:
- Deny always wins.
- Relative entries are workspace-anchored.
- Policies are evaluated against canonicalized real paths.

---

## Explicit Non-Goals / Out of Scope

- Exec sandboxing is not implemented here.
  R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”
- No shell argument parsing.
- Remote source governance is out-of-scope.

---

## Implementation Notes (for contributors)

### Policy evaluation edge cases covered

- `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit **deny-all** (not “no restriction”).
- Relative literal entries are **re-anchored after realpath**: if a workspace-relative entry canonicalizes outside the workspace (e.g. it points at a symlink to an external dir), it **does not** match outside-workspace targets.
- Absolute glob entries support symlink-alias authoring by **canonicalizing the non-glob prefix** before matching.
  - When rebuilding the rewritten pattern, the implementation **normalizes slash joining** so root-anchored globs stay semantically equivalent (e.g. `"/**/*.pem"` must not become `"//**/*.pem"`).

### Tool behavior on denial (model guidance)

When a filesystem access is blocked by PathGuard, tools return a structured `policy_denied` result (English text + structured fields) so models:
- do not attempt “bypass retries”,
- clearly communicate what was attempted,
- present actionable remediation options,
- and point to config keys (`tools.fs.*`) for operators to change if access is intended.

### Subagent filesystem ceiling (monotonic tightening)

Effective subagent policy is computed as a ceiling and is monotonic (cannot be widened by spawn calls):
- global: `tools.fs`
- agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- sessions-spawn defaults: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`
- spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` / tool arg `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge intent:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

The effective ceiling is persisted on the child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) and forwarded into the embedded runner (`toolFsPolicy` → `createOpenClawCodingTools({ fsPolicy })`).

---

## Verification

- CI checks and tests pass (per latest runs).
- Added/maintained coverage ensuring `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` is applied as a default tightening when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`.
- Verified critical denial scenarios: traversal, symlink/junction escapes, ENOENT parent-walk, deny precedence, empty allowlist deny-all, sandbox media enforcement, apply_patch enforcement, and subagent ceiling propagation.
- Verified monotonic nested spawning: a tightened subagent cannot spawn a child with broader FS access (parent `spawnedToolFsPolicy` is inherited).
- Verified gateway resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve the spawned FS ceiling (no widening after reset).

---

## Reviewer / Contributor Notes (Do not regress these invariants)

- Do not replace per-path enforcement with root-prefix approximations for glob policies.
- Keep deny-overrides-allow.
- Keep relative policy entries anchored to workspace (including after realpath).
- Keep sandbox and host local-file enforcement aligned.
- Keep local filesystem policy and remote-source policy separate concepts.
- Keep subagent policy tightening monotonic (no privilege escalation vs ceiling).
- Remember: exec is outside this enforcement layer.

---

## Rollback / Recovery

- Disable restrictions by setting `workspaceOnly: false` and clearing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths`.
- No data migration required.

target.title: Rescue: add watchdog core service and cron engine
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had no built-in isolated rescue watchdog that could detect an unhealthy managed gateway profile and repair it without going through the failing primary session.
- Why it matters: the larger rescue-watchdog feature is hard to review as one PR; this split lands the core runtime/service layer first so maintainers can review the actual watchdog mechanism without onboarding/UI noise.
- What changed: adds daemon/service hardening needed by the watchdog, introduces a new isolated `rescueWatchdog` cron payload + runner, and wires the gateway cron service to execute that watchdog job against a monitored profile.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no onboarding wizard flow, no CLI `openclaw onboard --rescue-watchdog` UX, and no docs changes in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #31480
- Supersedes #44113
- Supersedes #46493
- Supersedes #46499

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Adds a new internal isolated cron payload kind, `rescueWatchdog`, for watchdog jobs that monitor a target profile and repair it when unhealthy.
- Hardens LaunchAgent plist writes: trusted-home resolution, symlink rejection, atomic writes, and `0600` plist permissions.
- Managed service restart helpers now accept `AbortSignal`, so watchdog restart attempts can be canceled cleanly instead of hanging until command timeouts.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  The new capability is intentionally narrow: an isolated cron job can probe and repair a monitored local profile. Risk is bounded by restricting the payload shape, disallowing delivery targets for `rescueWatchdog`, resolving the monitored profile explicitly, and hardening the underlying managed-service restart and launchd plist write paths. The repair fallback uses exact argv via `process.execPath` and avoids shell-based PATH resolution.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): local managed gateway service / cron service only

### Steps

1. Run the daemon/unit tests covering launchd, systemd, schtasks, and process-exec timeout behavior.
2. Run the cron/rescue unit tests covering payload normalization, job validation, watchdog execution, and gateway cron wiring.
3. Build the repo and validate config to confirm the combined core layer compiles and loads cleanly.

### Expected

- A `rescueWatchdog` cron job can be normalized, stored, validated, and executed as an isolated job.
- Managed service restart helpers are abortable and launchd plist writes fail closed on unsafe targets.

### Actual

- Verified by targeted tests and local build/config validation; behavior matches the expected outcomes above.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - launchd plist path resolution ignores a conflicting `HOME` override, rejects symlink targets, and writes plist files with `0600` permissions via temp-file + rename.
  - `AbortSignal` now propagates through launchd/systemd/schtasks restart helpers into the process exec layer.
  - `rescueWatchdog` cron payloads normalize correctly, are forced to isolated session targets, and reject delivery/failureDestination config.
  - the rescue watchdog runner probes the monitored profile, attempts managed service restart, falls back to `doctor --repair --non-interactive`, and reports success/error summaries correctly.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `os.userInfo()` failure falls back to `os.homedir()`, and unresolved trusted home fails closed.
  - monitored rescue-profile names are rejected.
  - restart/repair flows respect cron timeout budget and abort behavior.
  - `launchd` temp plist files are cleaned up if rename fails.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - manual end-to-end watchdog repair on a real long-running host with an actually failing managed gateway.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/daemon/*`, `src/process/exec.ts`, `src/cron/*`, `src/rescue/watchdog-shared.ts`, `src/gateway/server-cron.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: launchd install unexpectedly failing on valid paths, or isolated cron jobs rejecting valid `agentTurn` jobs after the new payload kind was added.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: LaunchAgent path hardening could reject previously tolerated but unsafe filesystem layouts.
  - Mitigation: the checks are limited to symlinked/non-directory targets in the LaunchAgents path and have focused test coverage.
- Risk: adding a new cron payload kind could accidentally loosen validation or delivery behavior.
  - Mitigation: `rescueWatchdog` is explicitly restricted to isolated jobs, delivery is rejected, and schema/service/normalization paths have dedicated tests.
- Risk: watchdog repair code could leave stuck restart attempts behind.
  - Mitigation: abort propagation is threaded through the restart backends and the watchdog enforces bounded restart/repair timeouts.

target.title: fix(ollama): show all thinking levels in /think menu for dynamically-discovered models
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #93835

When an Ollama model with thinking capability is dynamically discovered (e.g. via local Ollama endpoint `/api/tags`), it is not in the configured model catalog. The `resolveThinkingProfile` function in `provider-policy-api.ts` was treating `undefined` reasoning as non-reasoning, returning only `["off"]` levels — causing the Telegram `/think` menu to show only "default, off".

## Root Cause

Two issues combined:

1. **`provider-policy-api.ts`**: `resolveThinkingProfile({ reasoning })` used `reasoning ? A : B`, treating `undefined` as falsy and returning the non-reasoning profile (off-only).

2. **Catalog mismatch**: Dynamically-discovered models store their `reasoning` field in `dynamicModelCache`, but the thinking system queries `buildConfiguredModelCatalog` which only includes configured models. So for dynamic models, `reasoning` is `undefined` when resolving the thinking profile.

## Fix

Change `resolveThinkingProfile` to only restrict to off-only when `reasoning` is explicitly `false`. When reasoning is `undefined` (dynamic model, unknown capabilities), return the full reasoning profile so all thinking levels (`off`, `low`, `medium`, `high`, `max`) appear in the `/think` menu.

## Behavior mapping

| Scenario | reasoning value | Before fix | After fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reasoning model (configured) | `true` | all levels ✅ | all levels ✅ |
| Non-reasoning model (configured) | `false` | off only ✅ | off only ✅ |
| Dynamic model with thinking | `undefined` | off only ❌ | all levels ✅ |
| Dynamic model without thinking | `undefined` | off only ⚠️ | all levels (consistent with other providers) ✅ |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram `/think` menu shows only "default, off" for dynamically-discovered Ollama models that have thinking capability and accept `/think medium` etc.

**Real environment tested**: Code path analysis on `openclaw/openclaw` main branch (commit `0c651fd082b`), reviewed against the full call chain:
```
/think menu → resolveCommandArgMenu → resolveCommandArgChoices
→ buildBuiltinChatCommands({ listThinkingLevels })
→ listThinkingLevels(provider, model, catalog)
→ resolveThinkingProfile({ provider, model, catalog })
→ resolveProviderThinkingProfile({ provider: "ollama", context })
→ resolveBundledProviderPolicySurface("ollama")
→ provider-policy-api.ts::resolveThinkingProfile({ reasoning })
```

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Code review of the diff and trace verification:

```diff
-  return reasoning ? OLLAMA_REASONING_THINKING_PROFILE : OLLAMA_NON_REASONING_THINKING_PROFILE;
+  if (reasoning === false) {
+    return OLLAMA_NON_REASONING_THINKING_PROFILE;
+  }
+  return OLLAMA_REASONING_THINKING_PROFILE;
```

**Evidence after fix**: The fix changes the conditional from loose falsy check (`reasoning ?`) to strict equality check (`reasoning === false`). This is the complete proof trace:

```typescript
// provider-policy-api.ts — the two thinking profiles:

const OLLAMA_REASONING_THINKING_PROFILE = {
  levels: [{ id: "off" }, { id: "low" }, { id: "medium" }, { id: "high" }, { id: "max" }],
  defaultLevel: "off",
} satisfies ProviderThinkingProfile;

const OLLAMA_NON_REASONING_THINKING_PROFILE = {
  levels: [{ id: "off" }],
  defaultLevel: "off",
} satisfies ProviderThinkingProfile;

// Before fix — reasoning=undefined → falsy → NON_REASONING → /think menu shows "default, off"
// After fix  — reasoning=undefined → !== false → REASONING → /think menu shows "default, off, low, medium, high, max"
```

Call chain trace:

```
[Scenario: Dynamic Ollama model "glm-5.2:cloud" with thinking capability, not in config catalog]

1. resolveThinkingPolicyContext({ provider:"ollama", model:"glm-5.2:cloud", catalog })
   → catalog.find(...) → undefined (model is dynamic, not configured)
   → returns { normalizedProvider:"ollama", reasoning:undefined, ... }

2. resolveProviderThinkingProfile({ provider:"ollama", context:{ reasoning:undefined } })
   → resolveActiveThinkingProvider("ollama") → undefined (plugin registry format mismatch)
   → resolveBundledProviderPolicySurface("ollama")
     → loads extensions/ollama/provider-policy-api.js
     → calls resolveThinkingProfile({ reasoning: undefined })

3. BEFORE FIX:  undefined ? REASONING : NON_REASONING → NON_REASONING_PROFILE → ["off"]
4. AFTER  FIX:  undefined === false? NO → REASONING_PROFILE → ["off","low","medium","high","max"]

5. listThinkingLevelChoices returns:
   BEFORE: ["default", "off"]                    → Telegram menu: "default, off"
   AFTER:  ["default", "off","low","medium","high","max"] → Telegram menu: full list
```

**Observed result after fix**: For any dynamically-discovered Ollama model, `resolveThinkingProfile` will return the reasoning profile (with all levels: off, low, medium, high, max) instead of the non-reasoning profile (off only). The `/think` Telegram menu will display `"default, off, low, medium, high, max"` as options, matching the actual model capabilities reported by `ollama/api/show`.

**What was not tested**: This fix was not tested against a live Ollama instance + Telegram setup. The fix is based on static code path analysis. The dynamically-discovered models `reasoning` flag is correctly set by `buildOllamaModelDefinition` (which checks `capabilities.includes("thinking")`), but this info lives in `dynamicModelCache` and is not propagated to the thinking systems catalog lookup — that mismatch is what this fix works around at the profile level. A more complete fix would also sync dynamic model metadata into the catalog, but that would require larger refactoring.

Fixes #93835

target.title: feat(cli): support --file for image generate command
target.body:
## Summary
The `image generate` CLI command was missing the `--file` option that `image edit` already supported. This PR adds `--file` support to achieve parity, forwarding input files to the image generation runtime.

Fixes #91734.

## Changes
- `src/cli/capability-cli.ts`:
  - Added `"--file"` to `image.generate` capability metadata flags
  - Added `.option("--file <path>", "Input file", collectOption, [])` to the `image generate` command registration
  - Wired `opts.file` through to `runImageGenerate({ file: files })`

- `src/cli/capability-cli.test.ts`:
  - Added regression test: `forwards input files for image generate`
  - Added regression test: `reports the expanded image.generate flags in capability inspect`

- `docs/cli/infer.md`:
  - Common tasks table now notes `--file` for `image generate`
  - Added `image generate --file` example in the code block
  - Notes section clarifies `image generate --file` vs `image edit` usage

## Verification
- `pnpm test src/cli/capability-cli.test.ts --run` — 97 passed
- `npx oxlint --import-plugin --config .oxlintrc.json src/cli/capability-cli.ts src/cli/capability-cli.test.ts` — clean
- `pnpm build` — clean
- `pnpm format:fix` — clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw infer image generate --file <path>` now accepts and forwards input files to the generation runtime, matching the existing `image edit --file` behavior.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux, Node.js 22, local source checkout, real test image file.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Verify `image generate --help` lists the new `--file` option:
     ```bash
     OPENCLAW_PROFILE=dev node scripts/run-node.mjs infer image generate --help
     ```
  2. Run `image generate` with an actual file path and prompt:
     ```bash
     OPENCLAW_PROFILE=dev node scripts/run-node.mjs infer image generate --file /home/0668000666/0668000666/AI/OpenClaw/test.jpeg --prompt "a blue sky" --json
     ```
  3. Inspect the published capability flags:
     ```bash
     OPENCLAW_PROFILE=dev node scripts/run-node.mjs capability inspect --name image.generate --json
     ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  Screenshot showing `image generate --help` with the `--file` option:
  ![image generate --help showing --file option](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/11ea03f0-611e-48be-9fb5-33fbb9f59a04)

  Screenshot showing `image generate --file` executed against a real local file:
  ![image generate --file execution](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/77b23b09-a281-4e66-b890-f4af18b39a5a)

  Screenshot showing `capability inspect --name image.generate` reporting the new `--file` flag:
  ![capability inspect showing --file flag](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/96f0acaf-5d79-4522-93d9-6f7b48cef55e)
- **Observed result after fix**:
  - `image generate --file <path>` is accepted by the CLI parser.
  - The file path is forwarded to the image generation runtime; the command reaches the provider dispatch layer and returns a normal "No image-generation model configured" runtime error, **not** an `unknown option` parse error.
  - `capability inspect --name image.generate` reports `--file` in the capability flags.
- **What was not tested**: Live provider image generation with actual API keys and model configuration; only CLI parsing, capability metadata, and runtime forwarding are verified.

target.title: fix(config): allow historyLimit: 0 in GroupChatSchema
target.body:
## Summary

`GroupChatSchema.historyLimit` used `.positive()`, which rejects `0`, while the other `historyLimit` schemas accept `0` with `.min(0)`. This made `messages.groupChat.historyLimit: 0` invalid even though `0` is the existing config value for disabling history.

Fixes #65305

## Fix

- Change `messages.groupChat.historyLimit` from `.positive()` to `.min(0)` in `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts`.
- Add focused runtime config schema tests for `0`, positive values, and negative values.
- Add a Gateway `config.patch` regression test proving the runtime API path accepts `historyLimit: 0`.

## Current Status

- Rebased cleanly on current `upstream/main` (`33b81686ad`).
- Current PR head is `a4f99c4cd2c05c26e52a06b315a5b81da24f509b`.
- Diff against current main is one commit: `git rev-list --left-right --count upstream/main...refresh-historylimit-zero` -> `0 1`.
- Main has not already fixed this: current main still has `.positive()` for `GroupChatSchema.historyLimit`.

## Test Plan

Current head verification after the final rebase:

- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts src/config/zod-schema.core.test.ts`
- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts -t "historyLimit: 0"`
- [x] `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/config/zod-schema.core.ts src/config/zod-schema.core.test.ts src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts`
- [x] `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `messages.groupChat.historyLimit: 0` must be accepted as a valid runtime config value so group chat history can be disabled.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout of this PR branch after applying the schema/test changes, built locally and run with a local Gateway using token auth.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built the branch with `OPENCLAW_RUN_NODE_SKIP_DTS_BUILD=1 pnpm build`, started the built local Gateway, connected through the backend Gateway protocol with `operator.admin`, read the runtime config schema, called `config.patch`, and read the config back from the running server.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the local Gateway API smoke run:

```text
helloAuth.scopes: ["operator.admin"]
schema.messages.groupChat.historyLimit: { type: "integer", minimum: 0 }
before: { valid: true, historyLimit: 1 }
config.patch: { ok: true, noop: false, historyLimit: 0 }
after: { valid: true, historyLimit: 0 }
```

**Observed result after fix:** The running Gateway advertised `minimum: 0` for `messages.groupChat.historyLimit`; `config.patch` accepted `historyLimit: 0` with `ok: true`; a follow-up read returned `valid: true` and `historyLimit: 0`.

**What was not tested:** The dashboard browser clickpath was not exercised; this proof targets the runtime Gateway/API config validation path directly.

This specifically covers the concern that direct config loading may accept a value while runtime JSON Schema/API validation may still reject it.
